title: Dog Walking on Tops of Paws: Causes and Urgent Care Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is walking on the tops of their paws (knuckling), it is a sign of neurological distress. This guide explains why urgent care is needed.
content_html:
What is Knuckling?

Walking on the tops of the paws, clinically known as knuckling, occurs when a dog is unable to flip their paw over to the correct position while walking or standing. Instead of walking on their pads, the dog drags or rests on the hair-covered top of the foot. This happens when the communication between the dog's brain and their limbs is interrupted, often due to nerve damage or spinal cord compression.

Is it an Emergency?

Yes, the urgency level for this symptom is High. Knuckling is frequently a sign of serious conditions such as Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), spinal trauma, or fibrocartilaginous embolism (FCE). Because neurological issues can progress rapidly to permanent paralysis, your dog should be evaluated by a veterinarian or an emergency specialist immediately. Prompt intervention often makes the difference in a dog's ability to walk again.

Why Photos and Videos Help Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a short video of your dog attempting to walk or a photo of the paw's positioning is incredibly helpful. These visuals allow the triage team to assess the severity of the neurological deficit and the specific limbs involved before you even arrive. This documentation helps the medical team prepare for your arrival and determines the speed at which your dog needs to be seen.

faqs.question: Is knuckling painful for dogs?
faqs.answer: While the nerve damage itself might cause numbness, the underlying cause (like a slipped disc) is often very painful, and dragging the paw can cause secondary skin abrasions.

faqs.question: Can a dog recover from knuckling?
faqs.answer: Recovery is possible depending on the cause and how quickly treatment begins. Options may include emergency surgery, strict crate rest, or physical therapy.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my dog to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as still as possible. Use a crate or a flat surface for transport to prevent further movement of the spine.
